projects.name: Development Methodology Transformation
projects.problem: SaaS team had no formal development process — shipped endlessly without releasing, lost momentum, vague tickets, disorganized meetings
projects.solution: Iterated through Scrum → Kanban → custom Scrumban framework tailored to the team's real constraints
projects.outcome: Delivered 100+ tickets in 6 months including a year's worth of stale backlog. Existing customers saw long-standing issues resolved.

projects.name: Multi-Platform E-Commerce Integration Engine
projects.problem: Needed to sync product, stock, order, and customer data between SaaS platform and 30+ webshops across 8 different e-commerce platforms
projects.solution: Designed SaaS as single source of truth with one-directional sync for merchant data, platform-specific adapters, and configurable rate limiting per platform
projects.outcome: 30+ webshops connected across 8 platforms with consistent data sync. Platform evolved further: brainstormed with CEO and CTO to build an in-house webshop builder, which I led as squad lead and was successfully launched as a selling product.

projects.name: AI-Driven SaaS Platform
projects.problem: Client had a startup idea to automate a multi-day manual digital marketing ad publishing workflow
projects.solution: Architected the core SaaS platform, optimized AI token consumption by ~50% while maintaining quality, led team from concept to functional product
projects.outcome: Transformed a multi-day workflow into hours. Made the product economically viable through token optimization.

projects.name: Offline-First CRM
projects.problem: Field operations teams needed a CRM that worked without internet and synced reliably when connectivity was restored, without losing concurrent edits
projects.solution: Designed snapshot-based synchronization with field-level conflict resolution — no data is ever overwritten, only appended
projects.outcome: Zero incidents of silently lost data. Full audit trail. The project earned recognition from senior leadership and accelerated my career growth.

projects.name: TechRostrum Digital IT Solutions
projects.problem: Wanted to build a software development business from scratch
projects.solution: Co-founded a solutions provider, architected client systems, led Scrum teams, managed client delivery and business operations
projects.outcome: Successfully delivered client projects while balancing engineering, leadership, and business ownership.

technical_opinions.position: Most developers underestimate how different each platform's API, data model, and behavior truly is. Shopify, Magento, and WooCommerce are not interchangeable — each has unique validation rules, rate limits, and edge cases.
technical_opinions.reasoning: I've integrated 8+ platforms and every single one had surprises. What works on Shopify will break on Bol.com. You need platform-specific adapters, not a generic API wrapper.

technical_opinions.position: Focusing on the happy path is the most common mistake in integration development. Even with extensive unit tests, there will always be scenarios you haven't covered.
technical_opinions.reasoning: E-commerce platforms fail in unpredictable ways — silent failures, misleading success responses, rate limits that change without notice. Plan for the unknown failures, not just the known ones.

technical_opinions.topic: Source of truth in multi-platform sync
technical_opinions.position: If you're syncing data across multiple platforms, you must establish a single source of truth. Bidirectional sync sounds flexible but creates unsolvable data conflicts.
technical_opinions.reasoning: At StoreKeeper, we made the SaaS the authority for merchant-managed data. Changes flow from SaaS to platforms, never the reverse (except for orders and customer-initiated data). This eliminated data conflicts entirely.

technical_opinions.position: Textbook Scrum rarely works perfectly in practice. Small teams, busy stakeholders, and frequent interruptions are normal — your methodology needs to accommodate reality, not the other way around.
technical_opinions.reasoning: I tried pure Scrum, it broke. Tried pure Kanban, it created different problems. Ended up designing a custom Scrumban that actually fit the team. Methodology should serve the team, not the other way around.

technical_opinions.position: Evaluate cross-platform tools critically upfront. If you'll eventually need native features, starting with Expo or React Native to save time may cost you more in the long run.
technical_opinions.reasoning: I chose Expo for a project because we lacked an iOS developer. Eventually had to eject and bring in a native developer anyway — the transition was more expensive than starting native would have been.

technical_opinions.position: I adapt my process to the client. For non-technical stakeholders, I run brainstorm sessions with my team where I come prepared with options and we reach consensus. For technical stakeholders (CTOs), I lead with RFCs and POCs before committing to architecture.
technical_opinions.reasoning: Not every client needs the same rigor. A startup founder needs to see a working prototype to validate assumptions. A CTO needs a documented decision record they can challenge.

technical_opinions.position: I treat the products I build as masterpieces. I want to look back at something and say proudly that I was once part of architecting or developing it. Software is art.
technical_opinions.reasoning: This isn't just philosophy — it means I go beyond what I was asked to do. I don't just close the ticket, I think about whether the solution makes the system better or worse over time.

leadership.style: Guide but never take over. Trust the team to grow. Create environments where asking for help is normal, not a weakness.
leadership.team_management: Led up to 11 developers. Assigned work, conducted 1-on-1s for onboarding and roadblock removal, performed PR reviews alongside the CTO. Focus on technical coordination and delivery rather than career management.
leadership.scope_management: Scope swap, not scope add. When stakeholders want to inject urgent work, I frame it as a tradeoff: 'We can do X, but Y gets pushed out. Which matters more?' I also build buffer capacity (~20%) into every sprint for inevitable scope creep.
leadership.conflict_resolution: When business wants everything shipped and engineering says it's not possible, I use my technical background to assess feasibility myself. I suggest simpler approaches, pair developers on complex tasks, or break work into smaller deliverables. I never sacrifice quality for speed — I'd rather deliver incrementally than deliver a fragile implementation quickly.
leadership.philosophy: Good communication leads to success. Even if you know what to do, you guide but never take over right away. When a developer struggles, I remind the whole team they can ask anyone for help — I never single someone out. Initiative and communication are the skills I value most.

about.career_narrative: I started as a PHP developer building government monitoring systems and marine automation software. Within two years, I was designing the architecture for an offline-capable CRM that changed the trajectory of my career. I moved into leadership roles — leading Scrum teams, introducing CI/CD pipelines, co-founding a software company, and eventually architecting multi-platform SaaS products for international clients. My path has always been: solve the hard problem, earn more responsibility, repeat.
about.current_focus: I'm looking for international Technical Lead, Engineering Manager, or Solutions Architect roles where I can combine architectural thinking, team leadership, and hands-on technical depth. I'm especially interested in companies building complex systems — SaaS, platforms, integrations — where the problems are hard enough to need someone who can think at the system level and still ship code.
about.differentiator: I go beyond what I was asked to do. You can always hire a cheaper senior dev, and there are developers better than me — I don't disregard that. But I treat every product I build as a masterpiece. I think about whether my solution makes the system better or worse over time, not just whether it closes the ticket.
